statement.markdown:
Given the root of a binary tree and a start node target, return the minimum number of steps to burn the entire binary tree starting from the target node.

In each step, the fire spreads to all adjacent nodes (parent, left child, and right child) of the currently burning nodes.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the tree.
The next N lines contain node values and their children, in the format: 'value left_child right_child' where left_child and right_child are the 0-based indices of the left and right child nodes respectively, or -1 if the child doesn't exist.
The next line contains a single integer representing the 0-based index of the target node from which the fire starts.
statement.outputFormat: The minimum number of steps required to burn the binary tree starting from target.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the binary tree.
The 0-based index of the target node.

examples.explanation:
Starting from Node 1 (val 2):
Step 0: Node 1 burns.
Step 1: Nodes 0, 3, and 4 burn.
Step 2: Node 2 burns.
All nodes are burned in 2 steps.

Starting from Node 1 (val 2):
Step 0: Node 1 burns.
Step 1: Node 0 burns.
Step 2: Node 2 burns.
All nodes are burned in 2 steps.

hints.text: Consider the height of the tree and how it relates to the minimum number of steps required to burn the tree from a given node.
Think about how the height of the tree affects the order in which nodes are burned, and how this relates to the minimum number of steps.
Consider the process of burning a tree node as a queue, where nodes are added to the end of the queue when their children are burned.
Think about how the given node u affects the queue, and how this relates to the minimum number of steps required to burn the tree.
Consider using a level order traversal (BFS) of the tree to find the minimum number of steps required to burn the tree from the given node u.

editorial.title: Burning Binary Tree from a Given Node
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the maximum distance from a given target node to any other node in the binary tree. A brute force approach would involve calculating the distance from the target node to every other node, but this can be optimized by observing that the maximum distance is equivalent to finding the diameter of the tree rooted at the target node.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve performing a breadth-first search (BFS) from the target node to every other node in the tree, keeping track of the maximum distance encountered. This approach is correct but slow, with a time complexity of O(N^2) due to the need to perform N BFS traversals.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach is to perform a single BFS traversal from the target node, keeping track of the maximum distance encountered. This approach takes advantage of the fact that the maximum distance from the target node to any other node is equivalent to the diameter of the tree rooted at the target node.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(N) and a space complexity of O(N), making it much more efficient than the brute force approach. This is achieved by using a queue to perform a single BFS traversal from the target node, keeping track of the maximum distance encountered.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that the BFS traversal visits every node in the tree exactly once, and keeps track of the maximum distance encountered. Since the tree is connected, the maximum distance from the target node to any other node is equivalent to the diameter of the tree rooted at the target node.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution on a sample test case reveals that it produces the correct result. For example, on a tree with nodes [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] and target node 0, the solution correctly outputs the maximum distance.
commonMistakes: Not using a visited set to avoid revisiting nodes
Not keeping track of the maximum distance encountered
Using a depth-first search (DFS) traversal instead of BFS
Not handling the case where the target node is a leaf node
Not checking for an empty tree
Not using a queue to perform the BFS traversal efficiently

functionSignature.java: public int burnTree(TreeNode root, int target);
functionSignature.python: def burnTree(self, root: TreeNode, target: int) -> int:
